title: Generative AI
desc: Master LLMs, prompt engineering, RAG, fine-tuning, and AI agents

sections.title: The Problem You Already Face
sections.content:
You're a software engineer. Every day you write boilerplate code, draft documentation, answer repetitive questions, and translate requirements into implementations.

What if a system could generate working code from natural language, write docs by reading your codebase, and answer user questions by understanding your product?

This isn't hypothetical — it's what LLMs do TODAY. But understanding HOW they work (and when they fail) separates engineers who use AI effectively from those who get burned.

sections.title: See It: The Evolution of AI
sections.content:
Each wave built on the previous. Classical ML needed hand-crafted features. Deep learning learned features automatically but still needed task-specific training. Foundation models learn general representations from massive unlabeled data, then adapt to ANY task.

The key insight: the jump from task-specific to general-purpose is what makes generative AI revolutionary. One model can write code, translate languages, summarize documents, and reason about math.

sections.title: What Generative Actually Means
sections.content:
All previous ML was DISCRIMINATIVE — it learned boundaries between categories.

• Is this email spam? → Yes/No
• What digit is this? → 0-9

Generative models learn the DISTRIBUTION of data itself.

• Write an email about... → generates novel text
• Complete this code... → generates novel code

The fundamental operation: given context (prompt), predict what comes next, one token at a time.

Three types:
AUTOREGRESSIVE (GPT, Claude): Generate left-to-right. Best for text.
DIFFUSION (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E): Start with noise, denoise iteratively. Best for images.
VARIATIONAL (VAEs): Learn compressed latent space. Used in drug discovery.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Discriminative models are like a LINTER — they classify code as good/bad. Generative models are like a CODE GENERATOR — they create new working code. The generator needs much deeper understanding.

sections.title: The Scaling Laws That Changed Everything
sections.content:
In 2020, OpenAI discovered SCALING LAWS:

Performance improves PREDICTABLY as you increase:
1. MODEL SIZE (parameters)
2. DATASET SIZE (tokens)
3. COMPUTE (GPU hours)

More surprising: EMERGENT ABILITIES appear at scale.
• GPT-2 (1.5B params): decent text generation
• GPT-3 (175B params): suddenly does arithmetic, translation, coding
• GPT-4 (~1.8T params): passes bar exam, writes complex code

These abilities weren't explicitly trained — they EMERGED from next-token prediction at sufficient scale.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Scaling laws are like Moore's Law for AI. Just as doubling transistors predictably doubled compute, doubling model size predictably improves capability.

sections.q: A model trained ONLY to predict the next word can suddenly do arithmetic at large scale. Why?
opts: It memorized all math from training data
To predict the next token in '25 × 4 = ', the model must learn arithmetic as an internal subroutine — emergent capabilities arise when large enough
Arithmetic was a separate training objective
sections.explanation: Next-token prediction is deceptively powerful. To predict that '100' follows '25 × 4 = ', the model develops internal circuits that perform multiplication. At small scale it takes shortcuts. At large scale it develops genuine algorithmic subroutines because they minimize prediction error most efficiently.

sections.title: Processing a 10,000-Token Document
sections.content:
You're building a system to analyze legal contracts. Each contract is 10,000+ words.

The old approach (RNNs): Process word by word. By page 20, you've 'forgotten' page 1.

The problem: A clause on page 1 directly affects page 20. The model MUST connect distant references.

Transformers solve this with ATTENTION — every word can directly look at every other word, regardless of distance.

sections.title: See It: How Self-Attention Connects Words
sections.content:
Watch how 'it' attends to all other words in: 'The server crashed because it was overloaded.'

Line thickness shows attention weight. Notice: 'it' attends most to 'server' — it learned the referent!

Different attention HEADS learn different relationships:
• Head 1: syntactic (subject-verb)
• Head 2: co-reference (pronoun → noun)
• Head 3: proximity (nearby words)

sections.title: The Full Transformer Block
sections.content:
A transformer processes a sequence through these steps:

1. TOKEN EMBEDDING: Each word → vector (768 dims)
   'server' → [0.23, -0.15, 0.88, ...]

2. POSITIONAL ENCODING: Add position info
   Without this, 'dog bites man' = 'man bites dog'

3. SELF-ATTENTION (the core):
   Q (Query) = 'What am I looking for?'
   K (Key) = 'What do I contain?'
   V (Value) = 'What info do I provide?'
   Attention = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d)) * V

4. MULTI-HEAD: Run h independent attentions, concatenate.

5. FEED-FORWARD: Two linear layers with ReLU.
   This is where 'knowledge' (facts) is stored.

6. RESIDUAL + LAYER NORM:
   output = LayerNorm(x + Attention(x))
   The '+x' skip lets gradients flow → enables 96+ layers.

Stack 32-96 of these → GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: A transformer block is like a microservice pipeline: • Attention = search/lookup service • FFN = processing service • Residual = message bus (preserves signal) • LayerNorm = load balancer (stable range)

sections.title: Why Attention is O(n²) and Why It Matters
sections.content:
Self-attention computes similarity between EVERY pair of tokens.

n tokens → n² scores per head per layer.

• n=1,000: 1M scores → fast
• n=100,000: 10B scores → very expensive

Solutions:
• Sparse attention (Longformer): O(n*k)
• Flash Attention: Same math, optimized memory. 2-4x faster.
• Mamba / State Space Models: O(n) recurrence.

Context windows: GPT-3 (2K) → GPT-4 (128K) → Claude (200K) → Gemini (1M+).
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: O(n²) is like a full mesh network. Sparse attention is hub-and-spoke. Flash Attention optimizes the protocol — same topology, faster.

sections.q: What would happen if you removed positional encodings from a transformer?
opts: 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' would be treated identically — attention is permutation-equivariant without position info
sections.explanation: Self-attention computes pairwise similarities. It's permutation-equivariant — shuffling input tokens just shuffles outputs. Without positional encodings, word ORDER is invisible. Positional encodings break this symmetry.

sections.title: When Your Model Can't Count Letters
sections.content:
Your AI consistently fails: 'How many R's in STRAWBERRY?' → '2' (wrong — it's 3).

Why? The model never sees individual LETTERS. It sees TOKENS: 'STRAW' + 'BERRY'.

Tokenization — how text becomes pieces — silently shapes EVERYTHING the model can and cannot do.

sections.title: See It: How Text Becomes Tokens
sections.content:
Watch how different text gets tokenized:

'Hello world' → ['Hello', ' world'] (2 tokens)
'Strawberry' → ['Str', 'aw', 'berry'] (3 tokens)
'print("hi")' → ['print', '("', 'hi', '")'] (4 tokens)

Common words = 1 token. Rare words = split into subwords. This is BPE.

sections.title: BPE: Building a Vocabulary
sections.content:
Byte-Pair Encoding builds tokenizer vocabularies:

1. Start with individual bytes (256 base tokens)
2. Count all adjacent pairs in corpus
3. Merge the most frequent pair into new token
4. Repeat until vocabulary reaches target (32K-100K)

Consequences:
• Common words → 1 token (efficient)
• Rare words → multiple tokens
• Code → 2-5x more tokens than English
• Non-English → more tokens per word (more expensive)
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: BPE is like COMPRESSION (gzip). Both find repeated patterns and replace with shorter codes. The vocabulary IS the codebook.

sections.title: Embeddings: Where Meaning Lives
sections.content:
After tokenization, each token becomes a VECTOR (768-12,288 dims).

Through training, embeddings organize meaningfully:
• 'king' - 'man' + 'woman' ≈ 'queen'
• Similar words cluster together

STATIC embeddings (Word2Vec): One fixed vector per word. 'bank' = same vector always.
CONTEXTUAL embeddings (Transformers): Different vector based on context. 'bank' near 'river' differs from 'bank' near 'money'.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Static embeddings = a DICTIONARY. Contextual embeddings = a COMPILER WITH TYPE INFERENCE — meaning depends on usage context.

sections.q: GPT-4 fails at anagrams and letter counting but excels at semantic tasks. Why?
opts: Tokenization splits text into subword chunks, not characters — the model can't 'see' individual letters
sections.explanation: When 'STRAWBERRY' becomes ['STR','AW','BERRY'], the model processes 3 tokens, not 10 characters. Individual letters within tokens aren't separate entities. Semantic tasks work because meaning operates at word/subword level, aligning with token structure.

sections.title: From 40% to 92% Accuracy in One Hour
sections.content:
Your team built a support classifier with GPT-4.

Initial prompt: 'Classify this ticket: {text}'
Accuracy: 40%.

After prompt engineering:
'You are an expert support analyst. Classify into: [Billing, Bug, Feature, Access, General]. Think step by step: 1) Identify concern 2) Match category 3) If ambiguous, prefer actionable. Ticket: {text}'
Accuracy: 92%. Same model, same data. Only the prompt changed.

sections.title: See It: Prompt Structure Anatomy
sections.content:
A well-engineered prompt has sections:

[SYSTEM] Role + expertise → behavioral baseline
[CONTEXT] Background info → reduces ambiguity
[INSTRUCTION] Task definition → what to do
[FORMAT] Output structure → JSON, categories
[EXAMPLES] Few-shot demos → show don't tell
[GUARDRAILS] Edge cases → prevent failures
[INPUT] Actual data → what to process

sections.title: The Five Core Techniques
sections.content:
1. ZERO-SHOT: Just describe the task.
   Best for well-defined, common tasks.

2. FEW-SHOT: Provide 3-5 examples.
   Best when showing pattern is clearer than describing.
   Key: examples should cover EDGE CASES.

3. CHAIN-OF-THOUGHT: 'Think step by step.'
   Best for math/logic. 30-50% improvement on complex tasks.
   Why: forces intermediate computation tokens.

4. ROLE PROMPTING: 'You are a senior security engineer.'
   Activates relevant knowledge neighborhoods in weights.

5. STRUCTURED OUTPUT: 'Respond ONLY with valid JSON.'
   Best for downstream processing.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Prompt engineering is API design. • Zero-shot = standard API call • Few-shot = example request/response in docs • CoT = verbose logging • Role = API mode/profile • Structured output = response schema

sections.title: Why Chain-of-Thought Works
sections.content:
A transformer has FIXED computation per token (same layers regardless of difficulty).

Chain-of-thought changes this. By generating intermediate tokens, the model gets MORE computation:

Without CoT: 'What is 47 × 38?' → [96 layers] → answer (hard)
With CoT: 'First, 47 × 8 = 376. Then 47 × 30 = 1410. Total = 1786.' Each step gets 96 layers.

More tokens = more compute = more reasoning capacity.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: CoT is like breaking a complex function into smaller functions. Each intermediate step is a variable between functions.

sections.q: Few-shot examples show clean invoices. In production, 30% are messy. Accuracy drops to 55%. Best fix?
opts: Add few-shot examples of MESSY invoices showing how to handle edge cases
sections.explanation: The examples created an implicit assumption: 'all invoices are clean.' Adding 2-3 messy invoice examples teaches the model the DECISION PROCESS for edge cases. This is the most common prompt engineering failure: examples only covering the easy 70%.

sections.title: Your Chatbot Confidently Makes Things Up
sections.content:
Customer asks: 'What's your refund policy for annual plans?'

Model responds: 'Full refund within 30 days.'
Actual policy: 'Prorated refund at any time.'

The model HALLUCINATED. RAG solves this: retrieve relevant docs first, then generate based ONLY on retrieved content.

sections.title: See It: The RAG Pipeline
sections.content:
Three stages:

1. EMBED: Documents → vectors (numbers capturing meaning)
2. RETRIEVE: Question comes in → find most similar doc vectors
3. GENERATE: Feed retrieved chunks + question to LLM

The LLM now generates from FACTS, not memory.

sections.title: The Three Pillars of RAG
sections.content:
PILLAR 1: CHUNKING
Bad: Split every 500 chars (cuts mid-sentence)
Better: Split by paragraph/section
Best: Semantic chunking with LLM
Sweet spot: 200-500 tokens with 50-token overlap.

PILLAR 2: EMBEDDINGS
Convert text to vectors where similar meaning = nearby vectors.
Models: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large, Cohere embed-v3, E5/BGE.
Key: Use SAME model for docs and queries.

PILLAR 3: RETRIEVAL
Semantic: embed query, find nearest neighbors.
Hybrid: semantic + keyword search combined.
Reranking: Retrieve top-20, cross-encoder reranks to top-5.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: RAG is a search engine for your AI. • Chunking = indexing • Embeddings = search index • Retrieval = search query • Generation = synthesized answer

sections.title: Vector Databases
sections.content:
Store millions of vectors, search in milliseconds.

Pinecone: Fully managed, serverless.
Weaviate: Open-source, hybrid search.
ChromaDB: Lightweight, prototyping.
PgVector: PostgreSQL extension.
Qdrant: High-performance, production.

Search algorithms:
HNSW: Graph-based, O(log n).
IVF: Cluster-based, O(n/k).
Trade-off: Speed vs recall. 95% of the time approximate = exact results, 100x faster.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Vector search is like DNS. You don't check every IP to find google.com. HNSW builds a hierarchical graph to find nearest vectors without checking all.

sections.q: RAG retrieves correct docs 90% of the time, but LLM gives wrong answers 25% of the time. Most likely issue?
opts: Chunks too large (irrelevant context confuses LLM) or answer spans chunk boundary
sections.explanation: This is 'retrieval good, generation bad.' Causes: chunks too large (LLM distracted), answer spans chunk boundary (half retrieved), or prompt doesn't say 'answer ONLY from context.' Fix: optimize chunks with overlap, add context-only instruction.

sections.title: When Prompting Isn't Enough
sections.content:
Your medical AI needs strict clinical guidelines. You've tried detailed prompts, few-shot, RAG with guidelines — it still occasionally gives dangerous advice.

The model's base behavior (helpful, general) conflicts with your need (cautious, domain-specific). Fine-tuning modifies weights to permanently encode new behavior.

sections.title: See It: Full Fine-Tuning vs LoRA
sections.content:
FULL FINE-TUNING: Update ALL 175B params.
Memory: ~1.4 TB GPU RAM. Cost: $10,000+.

LoRA: Freeze originals. Add tiny adapter matrices.
Instead of 4096×4096 (16M params), add 4096×8 + 8×4096 (65K params — 0.4%).
Memory: ~16 GB. Cost: $50-200.

Key insight: The CHANGE needed is LOW-RANK.

sections.title: When to Use Each Method
sections.content:
PROMPT ENGINEERING: Task well-defined, model already knows how.
FEW-SHOT: <20 good examples, learnable pattern.
RAG: Need private/current knowledge.
LoRA FINE-TUNING: Need new behavior patterns (100-10K examples).
FULL FINE-TUNING: Deep domain adaptation (10K+ examples).
DISTILLATION: GPT-4 capabilities at 7B model cost.

Decision ladder: Try prompting → RAG → fine-tuning → full training.
Only escalate when current approach truly isn't sufficient.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Adaptation hierarchy = software config: • Prompting = config file • RAG = external API call • LoRA = plugin system • Full fine-tuning = fork the codebase • Pretraining = rewrite the app

sections.title: Data Quality > Quantity
sections.content:
1,000 high-quality examples beats 100K noisy ones. Every time.

High quality means:
• CORRECT: Verified by domain expert
• DIVERSE: Covers edge cases
• CONSISTENT: Same format
• REPRESENTATIVE: Matches production distribution

Active learning loop:
1. Curate 500 examples → Train v1
2. Run v1 on unlabeled data
3. Experts correct mistakes → new training examples
4. Retrain v2. Repeat.

Catastrophic forgetting: Fine-tuning ONLY on domain data makes model forget general abilities. Fix: mix 10-20% general data.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Training data quality is like TEST QUALITY. 50 well-designed edge case tests beat 5,000 happy-path-only tests.

sections.q: You fine-tune LLaMA-7B on 5K medical Q&A pairs. It answers medical questions well but can't write emails or do math anymore. What happened?
opts: Catastrophic forgetting — weights shifted entirely toward medical patterns, overwriting general capabilities. Fix: mix 20% general data
sections.explanation: Catastrophic forgetting: exclusive medical training overwrote circuits for email/math. Solutions: (1) Mix 20% general data. (2) Use LoRA (keeps original weights frozen). (3) Lower learning rate. (4) Early stopping before general abilities degrade.

sections.title: From Chatbot to Autonomous Worker
sections.content:
A chatbot answers questions. An AGENT takes actions.

Your system must:
1. Read customer email
2. Look up account in CRM
3. Check order history
4. Decide: refund, replace, or escalate
5. Draft response
6. Send (with human approval)

This requires PLANNING, TOOL USE, MEMORY, and JUDGMENT.

sections.title: See It: The ReAct Agent Loop
sections.content:
The ReAct (Reason + Act) framework:

THINK: 'Customer upset about late delivery. Check order status.'
ACT: call check_order_status(id='12345')
OBSERVE: 'Shipped 5 days ago, stuck in transit'
THINK: 'Delayed. Check reshipping options.'
ACT: call check_reshipping(id='12345')
OBSERVE: 'Expedited available, 2 days'
THINK: 'Offer reshipping.'
ACT: generate_response(...)
DONE

sections.title: Building Reliable Agents
sections.content:
TOOL DEFINITION matters:
Good: name='search_orders', desc='Search by order_id, email, or date range. Returns status, items, shipping.'
Bad: name='search', desc='Searches stuff'

MEMORY:
• Short-term: conversation history
• Working: scratchpad for intermediate results
• Long-term: vector DB for past interactions

ERROR HANDLING:
• Tool fails → retry with backoff
• Unexpected format → parse gracefully
• Stuck in loop → max iterations + human fallback
• Dangerous action → require human approval
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: An agent is a microservices orchestrator. • LLM = orchestration engine • Tools = microservices • Memory = state store • Guardrails = circuit breakers

sections.title: The Reliability Problem
sections.content:
Each step has some error probability. If each is 95% accurate over 10 steps:
0.95^10 = 60% success. Not production-ready.

Solutions:
1. REDUCE STEPS: 3 instead of 10 → 86%
2. INCREASE PER-STEP ACCURACY: 0.99^10 = 90%
3. VERIFICATION LOOPS: Check each result
4. HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP: Approval for high-stakes
5. FALLBACK: Low confidence → ask for help
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Agent reliability = distributed system reliability. Compound failure probability is the same formula for chained service uptime.

sections.q: Agent retrieves data 98%, analyzes 95%, generates response 90%. End-to-end success rate? Most impactful fix?
opts: 83.7%. Improve generation (90→95%) — weakest link has biggest compound impact
sections.explanation: 0.98 × 0.95 × 0.90 = 83.7%. Improving weakest step (90→95%) raises to 88.4% (+4.7%). Improving strongest (98→99%) only gives 84.6% (+0.9%). Always fix the weakest link first.
